name: API paradigms & realtime
description: REST, GraphQL, gRPC, SOAP/RPC and realtime transports — choosing and defending an API style. From the 'API paradigmák' prep doc.

topics.name: GraphQL
topics.description: Query language with a single endpoint, client-specified fields, one round trip.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

GraphQL is a query language for APIs: instead of many fixed endpoints, there is ONE endpoint, and the CLIENT writes a query saying exactly which fields it wants — and gets precisely those, in one round trip.

## What problem does it solve?

Two chronic REST annoyances. Over-fetching: the endpoint returns 40 fields when you need 3 — wasted bandwidth, slower mobile apps. Under-fetching: one screen needs data from three endpoints — three round trips, or an ever-growing zoo of special combined endpoints.

## How it works

The server publishes a schema (the types and fields available); the client sends a query naming just the fields it needs — including nested relations — and the server's resolvers gather the data. One request, exactly the right response shape.

When it shines: many different clients (mobile, web, partners) each needing different slices of a rich data graph.

The costs, which the honest answer includes:
- Server complexity: resolvers and schema instead of simple handlers.
- Caching: plain HTTP caching mostly stops working (every query is a POST to one URL); you need application-level or persisted-query caching.
- Rate limiting: one query can be tiny or enormous — you must analyze query COST, not count requests.
- The server-side N+1 problem: a naive resolver loads each list item's relations one by one — a list of 100 fires 100 queries. The standard fix is DataLoader-style batching: collect the ids, fetch once.

REST stays perfectly right for stable, resource-shaped APIs with predictable consumers. GraphQL is a trade, not an upgrade.

## Key terms

- Schema / resolver — the type contract and the functions filling it
- Over-/under-fetching — the two REST pains GraphQL removes
- Query cost — why rate limiting must inspect the query
- DataLoader — batching that fixes the server-side N+1

topics.name: gRPC
topics.description: Contract-first RPC over HTTP/2 with protobuf, streaming, strong typing.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

gRPC is a high-performance framework for service-to-service calls: you define the contract in a .proto file, code generation produces typed client and server code, and messages travel as compact binary (Protocol Buffers) over HTTP/2.

## What problem does it solve?

Internal microservices calling each other over JSON/REST pay for parsing text, oversized payloads, and hand-written client code that drifts from the API. gRPC makes those calls fast, small and type-safe.

## How it works

Contract-first: the .proto file IS the API definition. From it, generators produce client stubs and server skeletons in any language — a Go service can call a Node service with full typing on both ends, and neither can silently drift from the contract.

Performance comes from Protocol Buffers (binary, much smaller than JSON, no text parsing) and HTTP/2 (multiplexed connections). gRPC also supports STREAMING natively: server-streaming (a feed of updates), client-streaming (uploading chunks), and bidirectional.

The limitations that complete the answer:
- Browsers cannot call gRPC directly — a proxy (grpc-web) is needed, so it is rarely the right choice for public web APIs.
- Binary payloads are hard to debug — no curl-and-read.
- External partners generally prefer REST/JSON with OpenAPI docs.

The typical architecture therefore: REST at the edge (people, browsers, partners), gRPC inside (service-to-service).

## Key terms

- .proto file — the contract; code is generated from it
- Protocol Buffers — compact binary message format
- HTTP/2 multiplexing — many calls over one connection
- Streaming — server-, client-, and bidirectional
- grpc-web — the browser workaround; a sign gRPC is for the inside

topics.name: Realtime transports
topics.description: Polling, SSE, WebSocket and webhooks for pushing data to clients.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The four ways to deliver events as they happen: polling (keep asking), SSE (a one-way server stream), WebSocket (a two-way connection), and webhooks (server calling server).

## What problem does it solve?

HTTP is client-asks-server-answers. Chat messages, live prices and status updates originate on the SERVER — some mechanism must get them to the interested party without constant asking.

## How it works

Sort them by direction and by who is on each end:
- Polling: the client asks every N seconds. Trivial to build, works everywhere, wastes requests when nothing changes. Fine for low-frequency needs.
- SSE (Server-Sent Events): the server keeps an HTTP response open and streams text events down it; the browser's EventSource API reconnects automatically. One-way (server → client), text-only — ideal for notifications, feeds, dashboards.
- WebSocket: a persistent two-way connection. Necessary when the CLIENT also sends continuously: chat, games, collaborative editors. Costs: reconnection is your job, and scaling is harder — each connection is pinned to one server instance (stateful), so multi-instance setups need a pub/sub backbone (e.g. Redis) behind them.
- Webhook: server-to-server — your system POSTs to a URL the other side registered, when an event happens. The standard for third-party integrations; brings retries, payload signing, idempotency and delivery logs as your responsibility.

Choosing: is the receiver a browser or a server? Does data flow one way or both? How much operational cost can you carry? Push beats polling as frequency grows — but polling's simplicity keeps winning at low frequency.

## Key terms

- EventSource — SSE in the browser, with free auto-reconnect
- Full-duplex — WebSocket's both-directions-at-once
- Sticky connections — why WebSockets complicate load balancing
- Webhook signing — proving to the receiver the call is really from you

questions.title: REST vs GraphQL
questions.prompt: How would you decide between REST and GraphQL for a new API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Anchor in client needs; over/under-fetching and one round trip vs caching, complexity and versioning.

expectedPoints.label: Frames around client needs
expectedPoints.description: Decides based on how varied and demanding the clients' data needs are.

expectedPoints.label: GraphQL solves over/under-fetching
expectedPoints.description: Client picks exactly the fields it needs in one request; avoids many round trips.

expectedPoints.label: REST strengths and GraphQL costs
expectedPoints.description: REST is simple, cache-friendly (HTTP caching); GraphQL adds server complexity, caching and rate-limiting challenges.

followUps.prompt: GraphQL can trigger an N+1 problem on the server. What is it and how would you address it?

questions.title: When to use gRPC
questions.prompt: When would you choose gRPC over a REST or GraphQL API?
questions.answerStructureHint: Internal service-to-service, performance, streaming and contracts vs browser support and tooling.

expectedPoints.label: Service-to-service performance
expectedPoints.description: gRPC suits internal microservice calls: binary protobuf over HTTP/2, low latency, streaming.

expectedPoints.label: Contract-first with generated code
expectedPoints.description: Proto files define a strict contract and generate typed client/server stubs.

expectedPoints.label: Knows the browser/tooling limitation
expectedPoints.description: Not natively callable from browsers (needs grpc-web/proxy); harder to debug than JSON.

followUps.prompt: gRPC supports streaming. Give an example where server or bidirectional streaming is the right fit.

questions.title: Realtime: polling vs SSE vs WebSocket vs webhook
questions.prompt: Compare polling, server-sent events, WebSocket and webhooks. When does each fit?
questions.answerStructureHint: Direction, who initiates, connection cost, and the classic use cases for each.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes by direction and initiator
expectedPoints.description: Polling/SSE are client-pull/server-push one-way; WebSocket is bidirectional; webhooks are server-to-server callbacks.

expectedPoints.label: Right use case for each
expectedPoints.description: Polling for simple/low-frequency, SSE for notifications/feeds, WebSocket for chat/live collaboration, webhook for third-party events.

expectedPoints.label: Mentions connection cost / scaling
expectedPoints.description: Persistent connections (WebSocket) cost server resources and complicate load balancing.

followUps.prompt: A webhook delivery can fail. How do you make webhook consumption reliable?
